edges.contract: T32 mints the container vocabulary PERMANENTLY. Under container-scoped trust every container carries its own trust declaration, so the at-rest shape T32 fixes must have room for it. Landing T32 first would mint a vocabulary that has to be migrated the moment this design lands - and it is a reserved-vocabulary mint, so that migration is expensive and public.

T34 stops being a hardening nicety and becomes load-bearing: the resource envelope is one of the two powers an operator CANNOT delegate (the other, erasure reach, is already built in #109). It is what makes hosting a delegated-trust container safe at all. T36 states that; T34 builds it.

tickets.title: Trust is container-scoped - admission delegates down, effectiveness attenuates (new SPEC §28)
tickets.body:
DESIGN-STAGE, and the largest open design in the arc - capability + federation + tenancy surface, so it is Myk's merge (P6) whatever it looks like. Deliverable: a drafted `spec/28-container-trust.md` plus answered questions, then STOP for Myk in chat.

ORIGIN (Myk, 2026-07-21, in conversation). Asked whether trust is a property of a delta's relationship to the OPERATOR or to each TENANT, the answer turned out to be neither: **trust is a property of a CONTAINER.** A store IS a container (the degenerate one - membership `input`, live, curated), so operator trust is simply the ROOT container's trust; tenants are containers within it, each carrying their own; and containers nest. This collapses four separate open questions into one primitive, and it collects a residual §7 has carried since step 13: 'per-tenant admin chains still mint community-vocabulary grants while constitutional strikes require store standing - revisit with trust-is-data.' Trust-is-data landed (`src/gateway/trust.ts`); the revisit never happened. This is it.

DECIDED ALREADY (Myk, same conversation - do not relitigate, write it down):

1. **Trust is container-scoped.** Not operator-scoped (which is what `trust.ts` builds today: ONE declaration at `loam:trust`, store-wide, operator-authored) and not tenant-scoped. Operator and tenant are two containers at different depths of one tree.

2. **The two axes must not be conflated - this is the heart of the section.** An early draft applied one rule to both and produced a design that would have forced operators toward blanket trust; Myk broke it with the Bob-federates-a-remote-source case. The corrected rule:
   - **ADMISSION** (may these bytes ENTER this container?) - what `loam:trust`'s open/roster/closed actually governs. **Delegates downward freely.** A child container may admit what its parent does not trust. Bob admitting Charlie's deltas into Bob's container puts NO pressure on Alice-the-operator's roster, because Bob's container is a distinct scope and Alice's ground is untouched.
   - **BINDING / EFFECTIVENESS** (does this law have FORCE?) - inert-by-default, §8/§12. **Attenuates.** Law admitted into a child binds within that child AT MOST, and reaches the parent only by PROMOTION (§24.3 adoption-merge, re-sign with `loam.adoption`, built #111).
   Note what the corrected shape IS: a child container is a QUARANTINE relative to its parent - a place where untrusted law may bind, inside and nowhere else, with promotion the only crossing. §24 is this design one level down, which is strong evidence it is right.

3. **The trust knob DETERMINES the boundary knob.** §27.1's 'spectrum keyed on trust' stops being a vibe and becomes a derivation: **a container that admits what its parent does not trust MUST be a WALL (separate store); a container that only narrows its parent's trust MAY be a PROPERTY (a flag over shared ground).** The reason is an escalation that would otherwise arrive by the back door - if a child were merely a SCOPE over the parent's shared ground, a child admitting a stranger would put the stranger's bytes in the parent's store, merely excluded from its default read. The wall is what makes delegated admission safe. This is §24.1's argument and the copy-knob rule (Myk, 2026-07-20) reaching the same place from two directions.

4. **What the operator keeps and cannot delegate.** The honest operator stake is NOT 'I vouch for your sources' but **'I can forget, and I cap the bill'**: (a) ERASURE reaches through every container unconditionally (§24.8, BUILT #109 - `erase` fans out to every attached pool, gated on the tombstone actually landing), so a child can never shelter a byte the operator must forget; (b) the RESOURCE ENVELOPE bounds what a child may spend (§24.5, unbuilt - T34). Those two are why delegated admission does not require delegated liability.

FEASIBILITY - ALREADY INVESTIGATED, DO NOT REDO IT (2026-07-21):
- **The admission half is buildable today, at any depth, with no substrate change.** Admission is NOT a lens: `admitForImpl` (src/gateway/ingest.ts) resolves the policy and returns a plain JS predicate. Walking a container tree to compose admission is ordinary host recursion.
- **The binding half hits a hard substrate ceiling.** `inView` is stratified at PARSE time - `term-json.js`: 'inView is stratified: no inView inside inView.term (SPEC-2 §3.1)'. So a LIVE, in-lens trusted set reaches exactly ONE link. That is why `lawfulStrikersJson` (src/gateway/accounts.ts) is written the way it is, and why §7's residual exists.
- **The workaround is sound**: resolve the container tree in TypeScript and BAKE the flattened result into the lens as a plain `inSet` predicate instead of an `inView`. Works at any depth today. It costs the property rhizomatic#2 was valued for - the lens stops being one live source of truth and becomes a snapshot the host must re-derive when trust moves. Loam already re-resolves trust and budgets from live deltas each request, so the cost is probably small; the risk is a host that forgets to rebuild and silently reads a STALE trusted set.
- Filed as **rhizomatic#27** (enhancement, not a blocker). rhizomatic is FROZEN - do not wait on it, and do not work around it in a forked vocabulary. Build against the bake path; if #27 ever lands, the live path is a later simplification.

QUESTIONS THIS TICKET MUST ANSWER:
1. Where does a container's trust declaration LIVE, and who may author it? Today `loam:trust` is one entity, store-wide, operator-only. Per-container needs a per-container declaration with a per-container lawful-author rule - and that rule is the recursion the bake path flattens. Say exactly how a child's admin standing is established without inView recursion.
2. Does the bake path need a STALENESS guard? A host that forgets to re-derive gets a silently stale trusted set with no error - an invisible failure mode, which is the kind this codebase usually refuses. Recommend a discipline (re-derive per request, as trust.ts and budget.ts do) and say whether it should be ENFORCED rather than documented.
3. What happens to §7's tenant machinery? It currently survives as 'vocabulary for author-communities and read lenses, not write fences.' Under this design a tenant becomes a real container with real trust. Is the existing vocabulary reused, or does it become a compatibility shim? A §20 migration question hides in this answer.
4. **THE DRIFT TO RESOLVE (Myk's call).** CLAUDE.md says 'v1 is fully multi-tenant - tenant isolation is first-class in the genesis schemas and gateway enforcement.' §7 says tenant machinery is NOT a write fence and entities are unowned. Those are different promises. Which is true today, and which should be true after §28? Do not resolve this by inference.
5. Does making tenants WALLS mean multi-tenancy becomes N backends rather than one store with N scopes? It follows from decision 3 whenever tenants do not trust each other - the normal case. State the operational consequence plainly rather than letting it arrive as a surprise at implementation time.
6. How deep may the tree go, and is depth bounded? §27.4 already rules that LIVE containers stay a TREE (drop-safety) and frozen ones may be a pinned DAG. Confirm that rule covers 'and so on' nesting, or name where it does not.

DO NOT decide question 4 unilaterally - it is explicitly Myk's.

edges.contract: Trust-on-load asks whether the smallest safe blessing is whole-module or PER-EXPORT. That question is unanswerable until the manifest has decided what an export IS and what it names — T31's recommended shape is a function of T30's answer to its questions 2 and 3.

The manifest is the second policy §27.7 waited for: it is what distinguishes a shippable module from a quarantine pool, and therefore what justifies lifting the hard-wired pool into a knob vector at all.

tickets.title: §27.6 q1: the manifest — where a container stops being a query's output and becomes a package
tickets.body:
DESIGN-STAGE. The first deliverable is drafted SPEC prose amending §27.6 (staged into spec/27-containers.md, which already exists — this is the rare edit-an-existing-section case, and it should read as the open question CLOSING, not as a new section) plus answered design questions. Then STOP and wait for Myk's word in chat before writing implementation code.

THE QUESTION, as §27.6 states it: a membership query says what is IN a container; a module still needs to say what it EXPORTS — which schemas / renderers / entities are its PUBLIC SURFACE versus internal deltas it needs but does not expose. Membership is decided and built (#132); the manifest is what turns a delta-set into a package with an INTERFACE.

DESIGN QUESTIONS TO ANSWER (each with a reasoned recommendation; none is reserved to Myk unless it turns out to touch capabilities, in which case say so and stop):
1. Is the manifest a SEPARATE artifact, or is it itself deltas inside the container? Strong prior: deltas — everything else in Loam is (trust is data, §8; a Schema is a publishable entity, §21; a container's knobs are claims about it, §27.1). If the manifest is a member delta then the module-version content address (BUILT — `Gateway.freeze`, `src/gateway/container-identity.ts`) covers it for free, and a module version pins its own interface. Argue it or overturn it.

ONE THING THE IDENTITY BUILD LEFT ON THE TABLE FOR YOU, AND THE SHAPE OF ITS ANSWER (Myk, 2026-07-20): a `ModuleVersion` currently holds its members as in-memory Delta objects, which is safe only because that is exactly what `select` already hands a caller and nothing is persisted. The moment a manifest — or anything else — PERSISTS a version, that changes: a stored version must not keep a BYTE COPY of its members, or an erased member (§11/§24.8) would survive inside it, and a stored version becomes somewhere a forgotten delta can hide.

The answer is HASH CONSING, which Loam already gets for free: content addressing IS hash consing, so a delta's id is its structural hash and the canonical instance is the one in the ground. So **a persisted version stores member IDS, never deltas.** That single choice is three things at once — (a) the shared representation, so a version over ten thousand members costs ten thousand addresses rather than ten thousand deltas; (b) the erasure guarantee BY CONSTRUCTION, because a version NAMES its members rather than holding them, so an erased member is simply gone from the ground the version points into and the version can honestly report it missing instead of harboring its bytes; and (c) the composition rule §27.4 already needs, since a version pinning another version is just one more address. Name things by their content address and never hold their bytes: the memory story, the erasure story, and the federation story are one sentence.

Carry that into the manifest's own shape — if the manifest is member deltas (question 1's strong prior), its exports should likewise NAME what they export rather than embed it.
2. What can be exported? §27.6 names schemas, renderers, entities. Is that the closed list? What about a resolver binding (§22) — it is law, and law is exactly what trust-on-load (T31) has to bless.
3. What does an export NAME? A raw delta id is content-addressed and brittle to re-authoring; an entity is stable but unowned. Recommend the shape a consumer writes when they say 'give me this module's Feed schema'.
4. Is a non-exported member READABLE by the consumer, or only unadvertised? Reference-load (§27.3) puts the whole container behind glass the consumer can read through by opt-in (§23.9/§24.2), so 'private' cannot mean unreadable without a read-side capability slice (§24.2's honesty note, explicitly deferred). Recommend what 'internal' honestly means in v1 — likely NOT-ADVERTISED rather than NOT-READABLE — and say so in the prose rather than implying a confidentiality the build does not deliver. This one matters most; the §24 arc has been careful never to promise a boundary it does not enforce.
5. Does the manifest carry a compat statement about what it needs from the HOST (a rhizomatic version, a Loam section)? Or is that a later concern?

WRITE IT so that a reader who has just read §27.6's 'Membership: decided. Manifest: open.' finds the sentence that closes it. Provenance footer per CLAUDE.md.

edges.contract: The `trust` knob is one of the five in §27.1's vector, and it is the knob that decides whether exclusion may be a PROPERTY or must be a WALL. T32 cannot set that knob correctly until T31 has decided what blessing a container's law actually means.

promote-LAW is the built form of the blessing T31 designs. If T31 recommends a per-export shape, T33 builds that shape rather than inventing a parallel one — so the design must land first or the two mechanisms drift.

tickets.title: §27.6 q3: trust on load — the smallest safe blessing for a module's law
tickets.body:
DESIGN-STAGE, and it lands on the CAPABILITY surface — so this PR is Myk's merge (P6) regardless of how clean it is. Deliverable: drafted SPEC prose amending §27.6 plus answered questions, then STOP for Myk in chat.

THE QUESTION, as §27.6 states it: a loaded module carries LAW (schemas, renderers, grants). Inert-by-default (§8/§12) says it binds nothing until blessed; 'install a module' is load + a scoped promotion of its law (§27.3, and §24.4 says promotion-of-law is the ordinary publish path remembering its probation). What is the SMALLEST SAFE BLESSING — the whole module's law at once, or a per-export grant?

Recommendation to argue from, not to assume: PER-EXPORT, because the whole-module blessing is the thing §6 spent the arc refusing — authority never rides in on data, and 'I trust this package' is exactly the ambient grant that makes supply chains dangerous. But argue the cost honestly: per-export blessing means a module with twenty exports is twenty operator acts, and an install flow nobody completes is its own security failure. The interesting middle is a blessing that is per-export in the RECORD (so an audit can say which export bound and who blessed it) while being one operator act at the UI (§24.7's promotion controls at the frame's edge). Say whether that middle is honest or a fig leaf.

QUESTIONS TO ANSWER:
1. Whole-module vs per-export vs the recorded-per-export/one-act middle. Recommend one.
2. Does blessing a module's law inherit §6's two-authority discipline — blessing the CODE and granting its PEN are different keys (§23.3, restated in §24.4)? Presume yes; state where the seam is for a module.
3. What does UNBLESSING look like? A blessed export is a published registration in the operator's own voice (§24.4), so withdrawal is negation — but the module version it came from is immutable. Does unblessing one export leave the rest bound? (This is where per-export earns or loses its keep.)
4. Does a module version pin the law it was blessed WITH — i.e. does upgrading to a new module version RE-REQUIRE blessing? Presume yes and say why: a new version is a new content address, so anything else is a silent authority upgrade.
5. What does the quarantine posture (§24) give here for free? A module loaded by REFERENCE into a container is already the probation story; blessing is promotion out. State the identity explicitly so the two do not drift into parallel mechanisms.

Do NOT decide a reserved '(Myk)' call unilaterally. If any question above turns out to need his sentence rather than a recommendation, name it as such in the PR and leave it open.

edges.contract: T32 refactors quarantine-pool.ts into the general Container. promote-LAW touches the same pool surface, so landing it first would mean writing the promotion path twice — once against the hard-wired pool, once against the lifted primitive.

The resource envelope is a per-CONTAINER budget. Attaching it to the hard-wired quarantine pool would hard-wire one more knob that T32 then has to generalize; after the lifting it is a knob on the vector.

The sequestered frame mounts a renderer against a container's handles and offers promotion controls at its edge. Both the handle scoping and the promotion door are Container surface, so the frame should be built against the lifted primitive.

tickets.title: §27.7: the Container lifting — quarantine becomes one preset of a named primitive
tickets.body:
The move §27.7 named and deliberately deferred until a SECOND policy wanted the same object. T29–T31 supply it: a module version is that second policy, and once it exists the quarantine's hard-wired knobs are an embarrassment rather than a simplification.

BUILD: generalize `quarantine-pool.ts` into a named `Container` carrying the §27.1 knob vector — `{membership, seeding, trust, boundary, identity}` — and re-express the quarantine as ONE PRESET of it (UNTRUSTED · one-way-seeded · live · droppable). `openQuarantine` keeps working, unchanged at the call site, implemented over the general primitive.

THE KNOBS, and where each already exists:
- membership — BUILT (#132): a Term, `QuarantineOptions.membership`, re-evaluated per pulse. Lifts as-is.
- identity — T29: static (freeze → module version) or live (re-evaluate → living container). This is §27.2's ladder, and the lifting is where the two stop being separate code paths.
- seeding — one-way inbound federation from a primary (built), explicit import of a shipped module (new), hand-authored deltas.
- trust — UNTRUSTED (foreign law inert, separate store) vs CURATED. This knob decides whether exclusion may be a PROPERTY or must be a WALL (§27.1's spectrum). Get this one exactly right; §24.10 is the normative reconciliation and it must not drift.

THE TRUST KNOB IS ALSO THE COPY KNOB (Myk, 2026-07-20) — a framing §27.1 implies and never states, and the lifting is where it should be said out loud. Read §27.1's spectrum as a STORAGE claim, not only a security one: a CURATED container is a query over SHARED GROUND — pure pointer arrangement, zero copies, the structural-sharing case, and the reason `select`/`freeze` hand back references into the reactor's snapshot rather than duplicates. An UNTRUSTED container is a SEPARATE ARENA — real byte copies (`openQuarantine` seeds a second backend by `federate(offeredDeltas())`), because discard-with-zero-trace and erasure-evasion resistance are precisely the things a view over shared memory cannot give you. So §24.1's 'you cannot discard a mark' is, underneath, the argument that ISOLATION IS THE ONE THING SHARING CANNOT PROVIDE, and therefore the one thing worth paying a copy for. The design consequence for this ticket is a rule, not a mood: **the Container pays bytes exactly where it needs a wall, and nowhere else** — a curated container that copies is a bug, and an untrusted container that merely filters is a hole. Rail both directions (the CURATED rail below already asserts no content address churns; add the dual — an untrusted container's members are genuinely a separate store, not a view).
- boundary — reference or merge (§27.3). Merge splits by trust: scope-merge (flip the exclusion property, NO re-sign) vs adoption-merge (re-sign with `loam.adoption`, built as promote-outputs, #111).

THE VOCABULARY DOES NOT EXIST YET, and minting it is part of this ticket. §27.1 says a container is itself an ENTITY and its knobs are CLAIMS about it, and that exclusion is 'a claim that container C is excluded' — but nothing under `loam:` or `loam.*` names a container today (grep confirms: no `loam:container`, no `loam.container.*`). So T32 mints reserved vocabulary. That triggers CLAUDE.md's rules in full: the shape must be unambiguously distinct from every prior version so shape-detection stays sufficient, and if it changes bytes older stores already hold it ships a §20 migration step in the SAME PR. Name the vocabulary deliberately and state it in §27.1's prose — it is the at-rest form of the whole knob vector, so a careless name here is one every later section has to live with. Follow the `loam:trust` / `loam:budget` precedent (a declaration re-resolved from live deltas, so changing a knob is a delta and not a restart).

SCOPE-MERGE IS THE NEW OPERATION and the one worth the most care: §27.3 decided it is flipping an exclusion claim on your OWN container, with no re-signing, because authorship never changed. It needs the composable set-algebra rhizomatic 0.6.0 landed (`difference`/`intersect` as first-class Term ops, adopted in T14) — the scope is `union of active containers MINUS the excluded ones`, nestable, which the old depth-1 `select(not(inView(T, id)))` idiom could not express.

RAILS:
- The quarantine preset is behaviorally IDENTICAL to today: re-point `test/gateway/quarantine.test.ts` at the lifted implementation and it stays green with no assertion softened. In particular §24.8's erasure law (tombstone + purge fan-out, the byte-level composed-scope assertion) must hold through the lifting, untouched.
- A CURATED container admits the property model: exclude it, the primary's read view loses its deltas; re-include it, they return; no delta was re-signed and no content address churned (assert the ids before and after are the SAME).
- An UNTRUSTED container refuses the property model — it is a separate store, and there is no flag that merges it. Assert the refusal, loudly.
- Nested exclusion composes: a container defined RELATIVE to another (difference against difference) resolves correctly. This is the case that was impossible before 0.6.0 and is the reason the algebra was worth adopting.

CARE: this is a refactor of a store-boundary/erasure/federation surface, so it is Myk's merge (P6) even though it is a build ticket. It must ship no behavior change to the quarantine — if it does, that is a finding, not a feature. §24's remaining slices (T33–T35) land ON the lifted primitive, which is why they queue behind it.

edges.contract: The frame's promotion controls offer BOTH doors — adopt an output (built, #111) and bless the law. The second door has to exist before the frame can put a button on it.

Ordering, not logic: the two are independent in design but the forecast scores them a scope-overlap VETO (both amend spec/24-quarantine.md's provenance and both land tests under the same quarantine surface). At width 1 they run sequentially anyway; this edge makes that explicit rather than leaving a known-conflicting pair nominally concurrent. promote-LAW goes first because it completes a §24.3/§24.4 story already half-built, where the envelope opens a new one.

tickets.title: §24.4: promote-LAW — the graduation is an ordinary publish that remembers its probation
tickets.body:
The second strength of promotion, on §24.9's DESCRIBE list, and the last unbuilt half of §24.3/§24.4. Promote-OUTPUTS is built (#111): a quarantine's FACTS cross by re-signing with a `loam.adoption` record, and it explicitly REFUSES law (a delta declaring a reserved vocabulary is rejected). This ticket builds the door that refusal points at.

WHAT §24.4 RECOMMENDED, and it is the whole design: promotion-of-law needs NO special quarantine machinery. It reuses the ORDINARY publish path verbatim — `publishSchemaClaims`/`loadSchema` for a §21 Schema, the §22 binding publish for a resolver, the §23 renderer binding publish for a renderer. The quarantine was the PROBATION; the ordinary publish is the GRADUATION. Blessing is a promotion out of a visible staging area, never a boolean flipped in the dark.

BUILD: `Gateway.promoteLaw(source, deltaId, opts?)` (name it as the codebase wants) which (1) recognizes what KIND of law it is looking at, (2) routes it to that kind's existing publish path under the OPERATOR's authorship — because foreign authorship binds nothing (§8/§12), the operator re-authors to bless, exactly as promote-outputs does for facts — and (3) attaches the quarantine record as PROVENANCE on the blessed thing: a `loam.adoption`-style pointer back to the pool id, the trial, who promoted, when. 'This law was vetted in quarantine before it bound' must be on the record and auditable like any other provenance.

§6'S TWO-AUTHORITY DISCIPLINE IS THE TRAP HERE, and §24.4 flags it: blessing the CODE and granting its PEN are DIFFERENT KEYS (§23.3). Promoting a renderer's law must NOT confer the granted author that lets it write. A rail must prove that: promote a renderer's law out of a pool, then assert it has no pen in the primary until a separate, deliberate grant. If the implementation makes those one act, the ticket has failed even with green tests.

OTHER RAILS:
- Idempotence, matching promote-outputs: promote the same law twice, converge on one registration and one provenance record.
- Erasure holds: a promoted law the operator later erased cannot be silently re-promoted past its tombstone (the same re-mints-the-same-id property #111 relies on).
- The blessed thing actually BINDS: after promotion, a read through the primary's governed door resolves THROUGH the promoted Schema/resolver/renderer. Assert the behavior, not the registration record — a test that only checks the record was written is hollow.
- Provenance survives: `Gateway.adoptions()` (or its law-side equivalent) shows the trail back to the pool.

CARE: capability + federation surface → Myk's merge (P6). If T31 (trust-on-load) recommended a per-export blessing shape, this is where that shape gets built — reconcile with it rather than inventing a parallel mechanism, and say in the PR which way the reconciliation went.

edges.contract: The frame runs a stranger's renderer for real, statefully, against real ground — which is exactly the workload the envelope bounds. Mounting that frame without a budget is the unbounded case §24.5 exists to close.

tickets.title: §24.5: the quarantine resource envelope — a budget that cannot starve the primary's doors
tickets.body:
**RE-WEIGHTED BY T36 (2026-07-21).** This was queued as a hardening slice. Under container-scoped trust (T36) it is one of exactly TWO powers an operator cannot delegate to a child container - the other being erasure reach, already built (#109). A child may admit deltas its parent does not trust; what keeps that safe to HOST is that the operator can still forget (erasure fans out) and still cap the bill (this envelope). Without it, delegated admission means an unbounded bill on someone else's say-so. Build it with that framing in the prose, not as a performance nicety.

§24.9's BUILD list item that slice 1 deferred. §23.9 built the confinement — an operator's bundle runs in a `worker_threads` Worker with a hard timeout and memory `resourceLimits`, bounding HANG, CRASH, and MEMORY. §24.5 says the quarantine needs that worker to run in ITS OWN POOL with ITS OWN budget, and states the hard constraint plainly: the quarantine's budget must not degrade the primary store's doors.

WHERE IT LIVES: `src/gateway/render-worker.ts` is §23.9's implementation and already carries the two comments this ticket acts on — its HONEST SCOPE note (a Worker bounds hang/crash/memory, NOT fs/net; true ocap is 'deferred to §24') and its 'v1 spawns a worker per render (~ms) … a small warm pool is the obvious follow-on'. The slot/concurrency knob below is that follow-on, scoped per pool. `src/gateway/budget.ts` is the neighboring precedent for the shape: door budgets are DATA, one operator-authored declaration re-resolved from the live deltas each request, so raising a quota is a delta and not a restart. Follow that pattern unless there is a stated reason not to — an envelope that needs a restart to tune would be the odd one out in this codebase.

BUILD: a per-quarantine resource envelope — the pool owns a worker budget (timeout, memory, and concurrency/slot count) charged against the QUARANTINE, not the primary. A stranger's app burning its whole envelope must leave the primary's doors answering at full speed.

THE RAIL THAT MATTERS, and it is a real one, not a proxy: with a quarantined workload deliberately exhausting its envelope (spin to timeout, allocate to the memory cap, saturate every slot), a concurrent read through the PRIMARY's door still completes within its normal bound. Make the rail concrete rather than impressionistic: establish the primary's baseline latency with the pool idle, then assert the under-load latency stays within a stated multiple of it (pick the multiple, justify it, and make it generous enough not to flake on CI — the failure this catches is 'the primary blocks until the quarantine's timeout fires', which is an order-of-magnitude effect, not a percentage one). Measure the primary's behavior under quarantine load. A test that merely asserts 'the pool's worker was configured with limits' is HOLLOW — it passes without the isolation existing. §18's door-busy work (T18, 'the door learns to say busy') is the neighboring machinery; reuse rather than parallel it.

OTHER RAILS:
- The envelope is per-POOL: two quarantines cannot borrow each other's budget, and one exhausting itself does not starve the other.
- Exhaustion is LOUD and attributable — the operator learns which pool hit which limit, not a generic failure.
- Dropping a pool releases its envelope (no leak across the drop that §24.1 promises is consequence-free).

THE HONESTY FLAG IS PART OF THE DELIVERABLE. §24.5 states it and the PR must not quietly outgrow it: until the full ocap layer ships (no-fs/no-net — SES-in-worker or isolated-vm, §23.9's named further work), a quarantine that admits rung (d) EFFECTFUL code bounds resource exhaustion but does NOT bound ambient-authority reach — a worker can still reach `node:fs` and the network. This ticket builds the resource half ONLY. Do not let the PR's prose imply the quarantine now safely runs a stranger's effectful app; that promise depends on the ocap slice, which stays deferred and should be re-stated as still-open in §24.5's provenance. Shipping the budget and overstating the confinement would be exactly the theater §24 exists to refuse.

NOT in scope: the ocap confinement itself. That is its own ticket once the SES-vs-isolated-vm choice is made — a dependency conversation, and it needs Myk.

tickets.title: §24.7: the sequestered frame — a stranger's app running against your real ground, visibly on probation
tickets.body:
The vivid case, and the one that makes §24 legible to a person rather than to a spec reader. §23 built most of the path: §23.3 decided a sandboxed renderer writes under a per-renderer granted author into the SANDBOX POOL, never canonical, and §23.9 built the confinement. §24.1 supplied the pool. What §24.7 adds is the FRAME.

BUILD, in the three parts §24.7 names:
FIRST, A CORRECTION TO CARRY INTO THE WORK: §24.7's prose says 'the stock React host (§23.2)', and that phrase does not map onto built code. There is no React host component in Loam. §23.2 is the host CONTRACT — the capability-scoped handles injected into a render function — and the serving path is `serveRouteImpl` in `src/gateway/renderers.ts`, which runs the bundle in the §23.9 worker and returns rendered output. So 'the frame' is chrome the SERVING PATH wraps around a quarantined route's output, not a React component to write. Build it there, and amend §24.7's wording in the same PR so the spec stops describing a host that does not exist.

1. HANDLES SCOPED TO THE POOL — the serving path, mounting a quarantined renderer, injects handles whose writes land IN the quarantine (§23.3's pen-into-pool) and whose reads see `primary-ground ⊎ its-own-writes` (§24.2). The point is that a probationary app is genuinely RUNNING, statefully: the operator watches it DO things, not paint a frozen preview.
2. A VISIBLY-SEQUESTERED FRAME — chrome that says without ambiguity: this is probation; its writes are LIVE HERE and quarantined from your store; promotion is the only crossing. §24.7 is emphatic about the failure mode: the frame must NOT say 'its writes go nowhere.' That would be a LIE — they go into the pool, and that is the whole point of a dry-run. A user must be fooled neither into thinking a probationary face is load-bearing NOR into thinking it is inert.
3. PROMOTION CONTROLS AT THE FRAME'S EDGE — bless the law (§24.4 / T33), adopt an output (§24.3, built #111). The only door out, and it is visible.

RAILS:
- A write from the quarantined renderer lands in the POOL and is absent from the primary, asserted at the store level, not the UI level.
- The app reads its own write back (it is stateful and live, not a preview) — this is the assertion that distinguishes §24.7 from a screenshot.
- Dropping the pool while the frame is mounted is consequence-free, and the frame says so.
- The frame's copy is itself under test for the §24.7 lie: assert the sequestration language does not claim writes go nowhere. (A string test is weak, but the failure mode IS a copy failure, so test the copy.)
- Erasure reaches through the mounted frame (§24.8 holds while a renderer is live, not only at rest).

THE VILLAGE (CLAUDE.md step 8): extend `demos/village/phase-quarantine.mjs` or add a new act so the village RUNS a stranger's face against real ground, writes into the pool, shows the frame, promotes one output, and drops the rest — end-to-end and visible. Update the demonstration ledger in `demos/village/README.md`. This is the act that shows what the whole §24/§27 arc was for; make it ambitious.

CARE: renderer + capability surface → Myk's merge (P6).
